nodes.name: Classic Novels
nodes.description: Foundational novels of world literature and their authors

items.prompt: Who wrote *Moby-Dick*?
items.explanation:
Published 1851; flopped commercially and was out of print when Melville died in 1891
Rediscovered in the 1920s 'Melville Revival' and now considered a masterpiece of American literature
Opening line 'Call me Ishmael' is one of the most famous in English
Based partly on the real sinking of the whaleship Essex (1820) by a sperm whale
Melville dedicated the novel to Nathaniel Hawthorne, his friend and neighbor in the Berkshires
Melville spent his final 19 years as a customs inspector in New York, largely forgotten

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Great Gatsby*?
items.explanation:
Published 1925; sold poorly in Fitzgerald's lifetime — fewer than 25,000 copies before his death
Became canonical after WWII when the U.S. Armed Services distributed free copies to soldiers
Set in the Jazz Age on Long Island; explores the American Dream's corruption through Jay Gatsby
Fitzgerald coined the term 'Jazz Age' and epitomized it with wife Zelda in 1920s Paris and New York
Died in 1940 at age 44 of a heart attack, believing himself a failure
The green light at the end of Daisy's dock is one of literature's most analyzed symbols

items.prompt: Who wrote *Pride and Prejudice*?
items.explanation:
Published 1813; originally titled 'First Impressions' when drafted in 1796–1797
All six of Austen's novels were published anonymously, credited only to 'A Lady'
Opening line: 'It is a truth universally acknowledged...' — among the most quoted in English literature
Austen never married; died at 41 in 1817, possibly of Addison's disease
The novel follows Elizabeth Bennet and Mr. Darcy — the prototypical enemies-to-lovers romance
Austen's face has appeared on the British ten-pound note since 2017

items.prompt: Who wrote *1984*?
items.explanation:
Published 1949; Orwell's real name was Eric Arthur Blair
Introduced 'Big Brother,' 'doublethink,' 'Newspeak,' and 'thoughtcrime' into the English language
Written on the Scottish island of Jura while Orwell was dying of tuberculosis; he died in January 1950
Sales spike every time a government surveillance scandal breaks — topped bestseller lists after Snowden leaks (2013)
Also wrote *Animal Farm* (1945), the anti-Stalinist allegory ('All animals are equal...')
Fought in the Spanish Civil War (1937) and was shot through the throat by a sniper; survived

items.prompt: Who wrote *To Kill a Mockingbird*?
items.explanation:
Published 1960; won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1961
Based partly on Lee's childhood in Monroeville, Alabama; Atticus Finch modeled on her father
Her childhood neighbor and friend was Truman Capote, the model for Dill in the novel
Lee published no other novel for 55 years until *Go Set a Watchman* (2015), written before Mockingbird
The novel has sold over 40 million copies and is a staple of American high school curricula
Lee was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2007

items.prompt: Who wrote *Crime and Punishment*?
items.explanation:
Published 1866 as a serial in a Russian literary journal
Follows Raskolnikov, a destitute student who murders a pawnbroker to test his theory of the 'extraordinary man'
Dostoevsky wrote it under crushing debt and a brutal deadline, sometimes dictating to a stenographer
Also wrote *The Brothers Karamazov* (1880), *The Idiot* (1869), and *Notes from Underground* (1864)
Sentenced to death by firing squad in 1849 for political activities; reprieved at the last moment
His epilepsy and gambling addiction informed much of his fiction's psychological intensity

items.prompt: Who wrote *Don Quixote*?
items.explanation:
Published in two parts (1605 and 1615); often called the first modern novel
Full title: *El Ingenioso Hidalgo Don Quixote de la Mancha*
Cervantes lost the use of his left hand at the Battle of Lepanto (1571) against the Ottoman fleet
Spent five years as a prisoner of war in Algiers before being ransomed
The novel satirizes chivalric romances through a delusional knight and his squire Sancho Panza
Cervantes died on April 22, 1616 — one day before Shakespeare's death (though different calendars)

items.prompt: Who wrote *War and Peace*?
items.explanation:
Published 1865–1869; set during Napoleon's invasion of Russia (1812)
Over 580,000 words with 580+ characters — a byword for length ('it's not exactly War and Peace')
Tolstoy also wrote *Anna Karenina* (1878), which opens: 'All happy families are alike...'
A Russian count who renounced his wealth and title late in life, embracing ascetic Christian anarchism
Influenced Gandhi and Martin Luther King Jr. with his philosophy of nonviolent resistance
Died in 1910 at a remote train station, having fled his estate at age 82 after a domestic crisis

items.prompt: Who wrote *Jane Eyre*?
items.explanation:
Published 1847 under the male pseudonym 'Currer Bell'
One of three literary Brontë sisters: Charlotte, Emily (*Wuthering Heights*), and Anne (*The Tenant of Wildfell Hall*)
The novel's 'Reader, I married him' is one of the most famous lines in English fiction
Features the 'madwoman in the attic' — Bertha Mason — a trope analyzed extensively in feminist criticism
Charlotte married in 1854 but died in 1855, likely from complications of pregnancy, at age 38
All three Brontë sisters died before age 40; their brother Branwell also died young, of tuberculosis and addiction

items.prompt: Who wrote *Wuthering Heights*?
items.explanation:
Published 1847 under the pseudonym 'Ellis Bell' — Emily's only novel
Shocked Victorian readers with its raw depiction of obsessive love between Heathcliff and Catherine
Emily died of tuberculosis in 1848 at age 30, just one year after publication
Kate Bush's 1978 debut single 'Wuthering Heights' hit #1 in the UK
The novel was poorly received initially — critics called it 'wild,' 'confused,' and 'disagreeable'
Emily was the most reclusive Brontë sister; she rarely left the family home at Haworth Parsonage

items.prompt: Who wrote *Great Expectations*?
items.explanation:
Published 1860–1861 as a weekly serial in Dickens's own magazine, *All the Year Round*
Follows orphan Pip from the Kent marshes to London; features Miss Havisham in her decayed wedding dress
Dickens also created Scrooge (*A Christmas Carol*), Oliver Twist, and David Copperfield
The most popular English novelist of the Victorian era, famous for public readings that drew thousands
Had 10 children; left his wife Catherine in 1858 for actress Ellen Ternan, causing a public scandal
Died mid-novel in 1870 (writing *The Mystery of Edwin Drood*); buried in Westminster Abbey

items.prompt: Who wrote *Catch-22*?
items.explanation:
Published 1961; originally titled *Catch-18* but changed to avoid confusion with Leon Uris's *Mila 18*
'Catch-22' entered the English language as a term for an inescapable paradoxical dilemma
Set among U.S. Army Air Forces bombardiers in WWII Italy; Heller himself flew 60 combat missions as a bombardier
Slow initial sales but became a cult classic during the Vietnam War as an anti-war satire
Heller's famous quip when told he'd never written anything as good: 'Who has?'
Took Heller eight years to write; he worked on it nights while holding an advertising job at Time magazine

items.prompt: Who wrote *Brave New World*?
items.explanation:
Published 1932; depicts a dystopia of engineered humans, soma drugs, and mandatory hedonism
Often paired with Orwell's *1984* — Huxley warned of control through pleasure, Orwell through pain
Huxley was nearly blind from age 16 after a childhood eye infection; read with a magnifying glass
Grandson of T.H. Huxley, the biologist known as 'Darwin's Bulldog'
Experimented with psychedelics; his 1954 book *The Doors of Perception* (on mescaline) inspired the band name The Doors
Died November 22, 1963 — the same day as C.S. Lewis and JFK's assassination, overshadowing both literary deaths

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Catcher in the Rye*?
items.explanation:
Published 1951; narrated by Holden Caulfield, the archetypal disaffected teenager
Still sells ~250,000 copies per year despite (or because of) frequent banning attempts
Salinger became a famous recluse after its success, moving to Cornish, New Hampshire, and refusing interviews
Published almost nothing after 1965; didn't publish a new book after *Raise High the Roof Beam, Carpenters* (1963)
Found on Mark David Chapman when he assassinated John Lennon in 1980; also carried by John Hinckley Jr.
Salinger served in WWII, landing at Utah Beach on D-Day and fighting through the Battle of the Bulge

items.prompt: Who wrote *One Hundred Years of Solitude*?
items.explanation:
Published 1967 in Spanish; has sold over 30 million copies worldwide
Follows seven generations of the Buendía family in the fictional town of Macondo, Colombia
The defining work of magical realism — blends the fantastical and the mundane seamlessly
García Márquez won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1982 — first Colombian Nobel laureate
He was a close friend of Fidel Castro, which drew criticism from other Latin American intellectuals
Worked as a journalist before fiction; reportedly sold his car to support his family while writing the novel

items.prompt: Who wrote *Beloved*?
items.explanation:
Published 1987; won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1988
Inspired by the true story of Margaret Garner, an enslaved woman who killed her daughter to prevent recapture
Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993 — first African American woman to receive the honor
Born Chloe Ardelia Woffard in Lorain, Ohio; 'Toni' was a college nickname from her baptismal name Anthony
Worked as an editor at Random House before her writing career, championing Black authors
In a 2006 *New York Times* survey of writers and critics, *Beloved* was named the best American novel of the past 25 years

items.prompt: Who wrote *Invisible Man* (1952), about a nameless Black man navigating American society?
items.explanation:
Published 1952; won the National Book Award in 1953 — Ellison was the first African American to win it
The unnamed narrator's invisibility is metaphorical — society refuses to see him as an individual
Ellison's only novel published in his lifetime; he worked on a second novel for 40+ years without finishing
Began writing it in a barn in Vermont in 1945 while on leave from the Merchant Marine
Ranked #19 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels of the 20th Century
Not to be confused with H.G. Wells's *The Invisible Man* (1897), a science fiction novel about literal invisibility

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Sun Also Rises*?
items.explanation:
Published 1926; Hemingway's first major novel, set among expatriates in 1920s Paris and Pamplona, Spain
Defined the 'Lost Generation' — a term coined by Gertrude Stein that Hemingway used as the book's epigraph
Hemingway's spare, declarative prose style influenced virtually all 20th-century American fiction
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954; also won the Pulitzer for *The Old Man and the Sea* (1953)
Was an ambulance driver in WWI, war correspondent in WWII, survived two plane crashes in Africa
Died by suicide in Ketchum, Idaho, in 1961, following a family history of depression

items.prompt: Who wrote *Ulysses*?
items.explanation:
Published 1922 in Paris by Sylvia Beach's Shakespeare and Company bookshop
Set entirely on June 16, 1904, in Dublin — now celebrated annually as 'Bloomsday' after protagonist Leopold Bloom
Banned in the U.S. for obscenity until the landmark 1933 *United States v. One Book Called Ulysses* ruling
Ranked #1 on the Modern Library's 100 Best English-Language Novels of the 20th Century
Joyce also wrote *Dubliners* (1914), *A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man* (1916), and *Finnegans Wake* (1939)
Lived most of his adult life in self-imposed exile from Ireland — in Trieste, Zurich, and Paris

items.prompt: Who wrote *Frankenstein*?
items.explanation:
Published 1818; subtitled 'The Modern Prometheus' — Shelley was only 18 when she began writing it
Conceived during a ghost story contest at Lord Byron's villa on Lake Geneva in 1816
Considered the first science fiction novel — the creature is animated by galvanism, not magic
Published anonymously; many assumed her husband Percy Bysshe Shelley wrote it
Her mother was Mary Wollstonecraft, author of *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman* (1792), a foundational feminist text
Shelley kept her dead husband's calcified heart wrapped in silk until her death in 1851

items.prompt: Who wrote *Madame Bovary* (1857), the novel about a doctor's wife in rural Normandy who has adulterous affairs and accumulates debt pursuing romantic fantasies?
items.explanation:
Published 1857; Flaubert was prosecuted for 'offense against public morals and religion' — acquitted
Emma Bovary, trapped in a dull provincial marriage, seeks escape through adulterous affairs and shopping
Flaubert's obsessive pursuit of 'le mot juste' (the right word) set the standard for prose style in the novel
'Bovaryism' — romanticizing one's life beyond reality — entered psychological and cultural vocabulary
Written over four years (1851–1856); Flaubert once reportedly spent a week on a single page
Considered the first great realist novel; influenced Tolstoy, Henry James, and nearly every subsequent novelist

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Trial* (published posthumously 1925), in which Josef K. is arrested without explanation and navigates a nightmarish, incomprehensible legal system?
items.explanation:
Published 1925, the year after Kafka died of tuberculosis; he asked friend Max Brod to destroy all manuscripts
Max Brod ignored the request and published *The Trial*, *The Castle*, and other works — an act that saved world literature
'Kafkaesque' entered English as an adjective for absurd, dehumanizing bureaucratic logic
Kafka was a Czech-speaking Jew from Prague who worked in insurance and wrote only at night
Also wrote *The Metamorphosis* (1915) — Gregor Samsa wakes transformed into a giant insect — and *The Castle* (1926)
Never married; had a fraught relationship with his domineering father, documented in the 100-page 'Letter to His Father'

items.prompt: Who wrote *In Search of Lost Time* (also known by its first volume, *Swann's Way*), a seven-volume novel famous for its passage on involuntary memory triggered by a madeleine dipped in tea?
items.explanation:
Published 1913–1927; Proust died in 1922 before completing the final three volumes, which were edited posthumously
Approximately 1.5 million words — one of the longest novels in Western literature
The 'madeleine moment': a bite of a tea-soaked madeleine triggers a flood of childhood memories — the defining passage
Proust's concept of 'involuntary memory' (mémoire involontaire) vs. deliberate recollection
In his final years, Proust was a near-recluse in a cork-lined Paris apartment, blocking noise and allergens
The title in French is *À la recherche du temps perdu*; an earlier English translation was titled *Remembrance of Things Past*

items.prompt: Who wrote *Anna Karenina* (1878), the novel following an aristocrat who leaves her husband for Count Vronsky, which opens with the line 'All happy families are alike; each unhappy family is unhappy in its own way'?
items.explanation:
Published serially 1875–1878; Tolstoy considered it his first true novel (regarding *War and Peace* as an 'epic poem')
Opening line is one of the most quoted in world literature — the 'Anna Karenina principle' is used in evolutionary biology
Anna's fate is foreshadowed early: she witnesses a railway worker killed by a train; she dies under a train
Parallel plot follows Konstantin Levin, widely read as Tolstoy's autobiographical stand-in, seeking spiritual meaning
Tolstoy, a Russian count, later renounced his novels, wealth, and title, embracing Christian anarchism
Also wrote *War and Peace* (1869) and *The Death of Ivan Ilyich* (1886)

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Brothers Karamazov* (1880), his final novel, exploring faith, doubt, and parricide through three brothers — Dmitri, Ivan, and Alyosha?
items.explanation:
Published 1880; Dostoevsky's final novel — he died in January 1881, months after completing it
Ivan's 'Grand Inquisitor' chapter is among the most influential philosophical passages in all fiction — a debate about free will, religion, and suffering
Addresses theodicy: Ivan challenges Alyosha with the suffering of innocent children as proof against God
Freud wrote 'Dostoevsky and Parricide' (1928) using the novel as a psychoanalytic case study
Also wrote *Crime and Punishment* (1866) and *The Idiot* (1869)
Dostoevsky was sentenced to death by firing squad in 1849; reprieved at the last moment and sent to Siberian labor camp

items.prompt: Who wrote *Lolita* (1955), the controversial novel narrated by Humbert Humbert, a European scholar obsessed with 12-year-old Dolores Haze?
items.explanation:
First published by Olympia Press in Paris (1955) — U.S. publishers refused; American edition appeared in 1958 and became a bestseller
Nabokov was Russian-born and wrote *Lolita* in English, his third language after Russian and French
The name 'Lolita' entered the language as a label for a precociously seductive girl, though the novel depicts predatory abuse
Nabokov was also a world-renowned lepidopterist (butterfly expert) who worked at Harvard's Museum of Comparative Zoology
Also wrote *Pale Fire* (1962) and *Speak, Memory* (1951), a celebrated autobiography
Adapted twice for film: by Stanley Kubrick (1962) and Adrian Lyne (1997)

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Stranger* (*L'Étranger*, 1942), the existentialist novel in which Meursault, emotionally detached, shoots an Arab man on a beach and is condemned as much for his indifference as the crime?
items.explanation:
Published 1942 in French as *L'Étranger*; Camus also published *The Myth of Sisyphus* the same year
Famous opening line: 'Mother died today. Or maybe yesterday, I don't know.'
Meursault's indifference to his mother's death becomes as damning in court as the killing itself
Central to Camus's philosophy of absurdism: humans seek meaning in an indifferent universe; one must imagine Sisyphus happy
Camus won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 — second-youngest laureate at the time
Died in a car crash in January 1960 at age 46; an unused train ticket was found in his coat pocket

items.prompt: Who wrote *Mrs Dalloway* (1925), a pioneering stream-of-consciousness novel following Clarissa Dalloway preparing for a party over a single day in post-WWI London?
items.explanation:
Published 1925; set on a single June day in 1923, unfolding through multiple characters' interwoven thoughts
Runs parallel to the story of Septimus Smith, a WWI veteran with shell shock — he and Clarissa never meet but mirror each other
Among the first novels to take shell shock (PTSD) seriously as a subject
Opening line: 'Mrs Dalloway said she would buy the flowers herself'
Woolf co-founded the Hogarth Press (1917) and published the first English editions of Freud's works
Died by suicide in March 1941, weighting her pockets with stones and walking into the River Ouse; left a note to her husband Leonard

items.prompt: Who wrote *On the Road* (1957), the Beat Generation novel of cross-country road trips based on the author's real journeys with Neal Cassady?
items.explanation:
Published 1957 after six years of rejection; became the defining manifesto of the Beat Generation
Kerouac reportedly typed the first draft in 1951 in three weeks on a single 120-foot scroll of paper
The scroll was sold at auction in 2001 for $2.43 million
Neal Cassady (Dean Moriarty in the novel) was also the driver of Ken Kesey's Merry Pranksters' bus in the 1960s
Also wrote *The Dharma Bums* (1958) and *Big Sur* (1962)
Died in 1969 at age 47 from an abdominal hemorrhage related to alcoholism

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Sound and the Fury* (1929), the modernist novel following the decline of the Compson family told through four narrators including the intellectually disabled Benjy?
items.explanation:
Published 1929; the first section, narrated by Benjy Compson, is one of the most demanding passages in American literature
The title is from Macbeth: 'full of sound and fury, signifying nothing'
Set in Faulkner's fictional Yoknapatawpha County, a detailed fictional world based on Lafayette County, Mississippi
Won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1949; also wrote *As I Lay Dying* (1930) and *Absalom, Absalom!* (1936)
Influenced Gabriel García Márquez and Toni Morrison, both future Nobel laureates
Worked as a screenwriter in Hollywood; received credit on *To Have and Have Not* (1944) and *The Big Sleep* (1946)

items.prompt: Who wrote *Their Eyes Were Watching God* (1937), the novel following Janie Crawford through three marriages in the early 20th-century American South, a cornerstone of African American literature?
items.explanation:
Published 1937; written in seven weeks while Hurston was on a Guggenheim Fellowship in Haiti
Initially dismissed by critics including Richard Wright as insufficiently political about racism; rediscovered in the 1970s
Alice Walker's 1975 essay 'In Search of Zora Neale Hurston' rescued Hurston's reputation; Walker had a headstone placed on her unmarked grave
Hurston was a central figure of the Harlem Renaissance alongside Langston Hughes and Countee Cullen
Trained as an anthropologist under Franz Boas at Barnard College; collected Black folklore throughout the South
Died in poverty in 1960, buried in an unmarked grave in Fort Pierce, Florida

items.prompt: Who wrote *Middlemarch* (1871–1872), the novel set in a fictional English Midlands town, often cited as the greatest novel in the English language, written under a male pseudonym?
items.explanation:
Published serially 1871–1872; Virginia Woolf called it 'one of the few English novels written for grown-up people'
'George Eliot' was the pen name of Mary Ann Evans — she adopted a male pseudonym to ensure her work was taken seriously
Central characters: idealistic Dorothea Brooke, who makes a catastrophically misguided marriage, and ambitious doctor Tertius Lydgate
Eliot lived unmarried for 24 years with philosopher George Henry Lewes (married to another woman) in a scandalous arrangement
In 1880, seven months before her death, she married John Walter Cross — a man 20 years her junior
Also wrote *The Mill on the Floss* (1860) and *Silas Marner* (1861)

items.prompt: Who wrote *The Grapes of Wrath* (1939), the Pulitzer Prize-winning novel following the Joad family's migration from the Oklahoma Dust Bowl to California during the Great Depression?
items.explanation:
Published 1939; won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1940; Steinbeck cited it in his 1962 Nobel Prize acceptance speech
Based partly on a series of articles Steinbeck wrote for the San Francisco News in 1936 on migrant farm workers
Banned and burned in several California counties as 'communist propaganda' and 'immoral'
Title is from 'The Battle Hymn of the Republic': 'He is trampling out the vintage where the grapes of wrath are stored'
John Ford directed the 1940 film adaptation starring Henry Fonda — considered a masterpiece of American cinema
Also wrote *Of Mice and Men* (1937) and *East of Eden* (1952)

items.prompt: Who wrote *Slaughterhouse-Five* (1969), the anti-war novel in which Billy Pilgrim, 'unstuck in time,' relives his experience as a POW during the 1945 firebombing of Dresden?
items.explanation:
Published 1969, at the height of the Vietnam War; Vonnegut himself survived the February 1945 Dresden firebombing as a POW
Vonnegut and fellow prisoners were sheltered in an underground meat locker — Schlachthof Fünf (Slaughterhouse Five)
The phrase 'So it goes' appears 106 times in the novel, always following a death — Vonnegut's fatalistic refrain
Introduces the Tralfamadorians, aliens who experience all time simultaneously and see death as just one moment
Also wrote *Cat's Cradle* (1963) and *Breakfast of Champions* (1973)
The novel was banned in several U.S. school districts; Vonnegut responded with characteristically sardonic humor

items.prompt: Who wrote *Things Fall Apart* (1958), the novel following Igbo warrior Okonkwo as British colonialism dismantles his Nigerian village's traditional way of life?
items.explanation:
Published 1958; the most widely read and taught novel in modern African literature
Title is from W.B. Yeats's poem 'The Second Coming': 'Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold'
Achebe wrote it partly as a response to European novels like *Heart of Darkness* and *Mister Johnson* that depicted Africa through a colonial lens
Has sold over 20 million copies and been translated into 57+ languages — the most translated African novel ever
Achebe is widely called the 'father of modern African literature' and won numerous prizes including the Man Booker International Prize (2007)
Lost the use of his legs in a 1990 car crash in Nigeria; spent his final decades at Bard College in New York
